heroOverview: Prediabetes is a metabolic state in which blood glucose levels are consistently above the normal range but below the threshold for type 2 diabetes — defined by the American Diabetes Association as a fasting plasma glucose of 100-125 mg/dL (5.6-6.9 mmol/L), a 2-hour plasma glucose of 140-199 mg/dL (7.8-11.0 mmol/L) on an oral glucose tolerance test, or an HbA1c of 5.7-6.4% (39-47 mmol/mol). Approximately 96 million American adults — roughly 38% of the adult population — have prediabetes, and more than 80% are unaware of it. Each year, 5-10% of people with prediabetes progress to type 2 diabetes, but structured lifestyle modification reduces that risk by approximately 58% and metformin reduces it by 31%. Prediabetes itself carries elevated risks for cardiovascular disease and microvascular complications, making early identification and intervention a clinical priority across primary care.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 96 million U.S. adults (38% of the adult population) have prediabetes; global prevalence estimated at approximately 541 million adults in 2021 (IDF)
keyStats.demographics: More common in adults over 45, those with obesity, family history of type 2 diabetes, gestational diabetes history, and racial/ethnic minorities — African Americans, Hispanic Americans, Asian Americans, and Pacific Islanders have higher prevalence than non-Hispanic whites
keyStats.avgAge: Prevalence rises sharply after age 45; however, increasing rates are now documented in adolescents and young adults linked to rising obesity
keyStats.globalCases: IDF estimates approximately 541 million adults worldwide had impaired glucose tolerance (prediabetes) in 2021, projected to rise to 730 million by 2045

definition: Prediabetes (ICD-10: R73.03) is the clinical term for impaired fasting glucose (IFG), impaired glucose tolerance (IGT), or an intermediate-range HbA1c — three distinct but overlapping glycaemic abnormalities that collectively signal insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction preceding overt type 2 diabetes mellitus. IFG reflects a defect in hepatic glucose production during fasting, while IGT primarily reflects impaired peripheral glucose uptake after a meal. HbA1c in the prediabetic range estimates average glucose over the preceding 2-3 months, is unaffected by acute stress or illness, and is the most convenient screening test. The condition arises from a combination of insulin resistance — reduced sensitivity of muscle, liver, and fat cells to insulin's glucose-lowering signal — and compensatory hyperinsulinaemia that progressively depletes pancreatic beta-cell function over years. While the term implies a transitional state, prediabetes is not inevitable progression: with intensive lifestyle intervention, many individuals return to normal glucose regulation, and this reversal is associated with restoration of beta-cell function. Even in the absence of overt diabetes, prediabetes independently raises the risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetic retinopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and chronic kidney disease. Specialist management falls under general internal medicine, endocrinology, or diabetology; however, most prediabetes is identified and managed in primary care.

typesClassification.description: Fasting plasma glucose 100-125 mg/dL (WHO: 110-125 mg/dL). Primarily reflects increased basal hepatic glucose output and early hepatic insulin resistance. Often asymptomatic and detected on routine blood tests.

2-hour plasma glucose 140-199 mg/dL on a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test, with normal or mildly elevated fasting glucose. Primarily reflects peripheral insulin resistance and impaired postprandial glucose disposal. Associated with higher cardiovascular risk than IFG alone.

An HbA1c in this range reflects average glucose elevation over 2-3 months. Convenient and reproducible. Roughly overlaps with but does not perfectly identify the same individuals as IFG or IGT — using all three criteria together maximises detection.

Individuals with both fasting and postprandial glucose abnormalities have the highest annual conversion rate to type 2 diabetes — approximately 15-20% per year — and are the highest-priority group for intensive intervention.

primarySymptoms: Prediabetes is typically asymptomatic, which is why population screening is essential. Most people discover it through a routine blood test.
Increased thirst (polydipsia) in some individuals when postprandial glucose levels rise high enough to spill into the urine, pulling water with it osmotically.
Increased urinary frequency (polyuria) for the same osmotic reason — glucose in the urine draws water and increases urine volume, although this is more characteristic of overt diabetes.
Unusual or persistent fatigue that is disproportionate to activity level, reflecting inefficient cellular glucose uptake due to insulin resistance.
Increased hunger, particularly in the hours after meals, as peripheral tissues fail to efficiently absorb glucose and signal satiety properly.
Blurred vision in some people when blood glucose fluctuates and causes transient lens swelling that alters refraction.
Slow wound healing or minor skin infections that take longer to resolve than expected, reflecting early immune function impairment from glucose-driven oxidative stress.
Acanthosis nigricans — dark, velvety skin patches typically at the back of the neck, armpits, or groin — a visible cutaneous sign of significant underlying insulin resistance.
Tingling or mild numbness in the feet, which suggests early peripheral neuropathy and indicates glucose levels have been persistently elevated long enough to affect small nerve fibres.
Unintended weight gain or difficulty losing weight in the setting of caloric restriction, due to hyperinsulinaemia promoting fat storage.
earlyWarningSigns: A fasting blood glucose reported as 'high normal' or flagged between 100 and 125 mg/dL on a routine panel.
An HbA1c returned as 5.7% or above on a general metabolic screen, even without any symptoms.
Development of acanthosis nigricans at the nape of the neck or armpits in an individual with central obesity.
A history of gestational diabetes, which predicts a 50% lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes and warrants annual glucose monitoring.
emergencySigns: Fasting blood glucose above 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L) with vomiting, abdominal pain, and lethargy — possible diabetic ketoacidosis (more typical of type 1 diabetes onset but occasionally occurs in metabolic stress) — seek emergency care.

Sudden onset of confusion, profuse sweating, trembling, or loss of consciousness in a person on metformin or other glucose-lowering medications — hypoglycaemia must be excluded; give oral glucose if the person can swallow and call emergency services.
Symptoms of a new myocardial infarction or stroke — prediabetes substantially raises cardiovascular risk, and these events require emergency care regardless of glycaemic status.
Signs of hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (extreme thirst, very high glucose readings above 600 mg/dL, profound weakness, confusion) — a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalisation.

causes.description: Skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue develop impaired responsiveness to insulin's signal to take up glucose from the blood. The pancreatic beta cells compensate by secreting more insulin; as long as this compensation is adequate, glucose remains normal, but when beta-cell capacity is overwhelmed, fasting and postprandial glucose rise into the prediabetic range.

Even in the prediabetic stage, beta-cell mass and first-phase insulin secretion are measurably reduced — by approximately 50% at the time type 2 diabetes is diagnosed, according to UK Prospective Diabetes Study data. This decline begins years before diagnosis and is driven by glucose toxicity, lipotoxicity, inflammatory cytokines, and possibly endoplasmic reticulum stress.

Visceral adipose tissue is metabolically active, releasing free fatty acids and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6, resistin) that directly impair insulin signalling in liver and muscle. Body mass index and waist circumference correlate strongly with insulin resistance; even modest weight loss of 5-10% of body weight substantially improves insulin sensitivity.

Skeletal muscle is responsible for approximately 80% of insulin-stimulated glucose disposal. Regular aerobic and resistance exercise independently improves insulin sensitivity and glucose transporter-4 expression for 24-72 hours post-exercise. Sedentary behaviour is one of the strongest modifiable predictors of prediabetes and its progression to type 2 diabetes.

Prediabetes has strong polygenic heritability; having a first-degree relative with type 2 diabetes roughly doubles the risk. Identified genetic variants include those affecting beta-cell function (TCF7L2, KCNJ11) and insulin secretion regulation. Ethnicity modifies risk substantially, with South Asian, East Asian, African, and Hispanic populations developing metabolic complications at lower BMI thresholds.

Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone, dexamethasone) induce insulin resistance and impair beta-cell function, causing steroid-induced hyperglycaemia that progresses to prediabetes and frank diabetes in susceptible individuals. Antipsychotic medications — particularly olanzapine and clozapine — also strongly promote insulin resistance and weight gain. Tacrolimus and other calcineurin inhibitors directly impair beta-cell function.

Short sleep duration (below 6 hours per night) and obstructive sleep apnoea independently increase insulin resistance through activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and sympathetic nervous system, raising cortisol and catecholamines that oppose insulin action. Shift workers have approximately 1.5 times the risk of prediabetes compared to day workers.

riskFactors.factor: Overweight and obesity (BMI ≥ 25 kg/m², or ≥ 23 kg/m² in Asian adults)
riskFactors.description: Central adiposity is the single strongest modifiable risk factor; visceral fat releases free fatty acids and inflammatory mediators that drive insulin resistance. A 5-10% reduction in body weight reduces prediabetes-to-diabetes progression by approximately 58%.

Less than 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity doubles the risk of prediabetes progression. Exercise improves peripheral insulin sensitivity within days of initiation; its benefits are dose-dependent and additive to dietary change.

A first-degree relative with type 2 diabetes approximately doubles lifetime risk. Polygenic risk scores improve prediction modestly over family history alone and may guide earlier and more intensive intervention.

Insulin resistance and beta-cell function both decline with ageing, and abdominal fat redistribution increases even in weight-stable individuals. Adults over 45 should be screened for prediabetes at minimum every 3 years regardless of other risk factors.

Women with GDM have a 50% cumulative lifetime risk of developing type 2 diabetes. Annual fasting glucose or HbA1c testing is recommended after a GDM pregnancy, as the postpartum period is a critical opportunity to intervene before further progression.

African Americans, Hispanic Americans, Asian Americans, and Pacific Islanders develop prediabetes and type 2 diabetes at substantially higher rates than non-Hispanic white Americans, and at lower BMI thresholds. ADA guidelines recommend lower BMI cut-offs for screening in Asian populations.

PCOS is associated with significant insulin resistance independent of body weight; women with PCOS have a 2-3 fold higher risk of impaired glucose tolerance. Annual glucose screening is recommended for all women with PCOS.

riskFactors.factor: Obstructive sleep apnoea and short sleep duration
riskFactors.description: Sleep apnoea and sleep deprivation independently impair insulin sensitivity through adrenocortical and sympathetic activation. Treating sleep apnoea with CPAP modestly but significantly improves glucose metabolism.

The metabolic syndrome — central obesity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and impaired fasting glucose — clusters in individuals with insulin resistance. Hypertension and cardiovascular disease both strongly predict concurrent prediabetes.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 45 — prevalence exceeds 40% in this age group in U.S. surveys
Individuals with obesity or overweight, particularly central adiposity
First-degree relatives of people with type 2 diabetes
Women with a history of gestational diabetes mellitus
Racial and ethnic minorities — African Americans, Hispanic Americans, Asian Americans, and Pacific Islanders
Individuals on long-term corticosteroids, antipsychotics, or calcineurin inhibitors
Those with obstructive sleep apnoea or very short sleep duration
diagnosisOverview: Prediabetes is almost always asymptomatic and discovered through targeted screening. The ADA recommends screening all overweight or obese adults (BMI ≥ 25 kg/m²) with at least one additional risk factor at any age, and all adults starting at age 45 regardless of weight, repeated every 3 years if results are normal. Screening tests are fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, or the 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT); each has slightly different diagnostic thresholds and identifies partially overlapping populations, so clinicians may use more than one. A positive screening result should be confirmed with a repeat test on a separate day unless two concordant tests are positive simultaneously. HbA1c is the most convenient, requiring no fasting, but is affected by haemoglobin variants, haemolytic anaemia, and iron deficiency, which can falsely alter the result. The OGTT is the gold standard for identifying IGT but is time-consuming and less reproducible. A structured risk-scoring tool such as the ADA Risk Test can prioritise which patients to screen with a blood test. Concurrent assessment for cardiovascular risk factors — blood pressure, lipid panel, kidney function, and urine albumin — is part of the complete evaluation, as metabolic syndrome components frequently co-occur.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most widely used screening and diagnostic test; prediabetes is defined as 100-125 mg/dL (5.6-6.9 mmol/L); requires an 8-hour fast to ensure accuracy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from the arm after an overnight fast. Results are available the same day or within hours. A repeat test on a different day confirms the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2-3 months; prediabetes is 5.7-6.4% (39-47 mmol/mol); no fasting required and highly reproducible.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw at any time of day. Results are available within hours to a day. Conditions that shorten or lengthen red blood cell lifespan can affect the result.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures fasting and 2-hour glucose after a 75 g glucose load; detects IGT (140-199 mg/dL at 2 hours) and identifies postprandial dysregulation that FPG may miss; the reference standard for prediabetes diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After an overnight fast, a baseline blood draw is taken, then you drink a glucose solution; a second draw occurs at 2 hours. The test takes about 2 hours and requires staying at the clinic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies dyslipidaemia (elevated triglycerides, low HDL cholesterol) that commonly accompanies prediabetes as part of metabolic syndrome, guiding cardiovascular risk management alongside glucose results.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard fasting blood draw; results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for early diabetic nephropathy even in the prediabetic range, as microalbuminuria can appear before overt diabetes. A UACR above 30 mg/g prompts more intensive blood pressure and metabolic management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A spot urine sample (first morning void preferred) provided in a small container; results available within a day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Calculates insulin resistance (Homeostasis Model Assessment — Insulin Resistance = fasting insulin × fasting glucose / 22.5) to quantify the degree of insulin resistance, useful in clinical research and in assessing PCOS-associated metabolic dysfunction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Combined fasting blood draw alongside the FPG; HOMA-IR above 2.5-3.0 suggests significant insulin resistance.

treatmentOverview: The Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP), the landmark NEJM-published trial in 3,234 adults with prediabetes, established that an intensive lifestyle programme achieving at least 7% weight loss and 150 minutes per week of moderate exercise reduced the 3-year incidence of type 2 diabetes by 58% — nearly twice the benefit of metformin (31% reduction). This lifestyle effect is durable and has been replicated in multiple national programmes. The U.S. CDC-recognised Diabetes Prevention Programme is now a structured, evidence-based group programme available in person and online, covered by Medicare and many insurers. The primary goals of treatment are to achieve and maintain modest weight loss (5-10% of body weight), increase physical activity, optimise dietary quality — particularly by reducing refined carbohydrates and saturated fat — and manage co-existing cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension and dyslipidaemia. Pharmacological intervention with metformin (1,000-1,700 mg daily in divided doses) is appropriate for adults with prediabetes who are under 60, have BMI ≥ 35 kg/m², and have one or more additional risk factors, or when lifestyle interventions have not prevented progression. Metformin's effect is modest compared to lifestyle change, is best sustained long-term, and is safe with careful monitoring of vitamin B12 and kidney function. Emerging pharmacological agents — GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors — are not yet approved for prediabetes but show significant glucose-lowering and weight-reduction effects and are under evaluation. Annual monitoring of HbA1c or fasting glucose is recommended in all people with prediabetes to detect progression to type 2 diabetes and to assess response to intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Intensive lifestyle modification (CDC Diabetes Prevention Programme)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured programme targeting at least 7% body weight loss through dietary changes and at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity physical activity. Delivered over 1 year in group sessions; available in person and digitally. The single most effective intervention for preventing progression to type 2 diabetes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 3-year incidence of type 2 diabetes by approximately 58% compared to placebo in the DPP trial; sustained reduction of 34% at 10-year follow-up in the DPP Outcomes Study.

medicalTreatments.name: Metformin
medicalTreatments.description: An oral biguanide that reduces hepatic glucose production and modestly improves peripheral insulin sensitivity. Indicated for adults under 60 with prediabetes and BMI ≥ 35 kg/m², women with a history of gestational diabetes, or those who do not respond to lifestyle intervention. Starting dose 500-850 mg with meals, titrated to 1,000-1,700 mg daily as tolerated. Monitor renal function and vitamin B12 annually.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 3-year incidence of type 2 diabetes by approximately 31% in the DPP; most effective in adults under 45 with higher baseline glucose and obesity.

medicalTreatments.name: Statin therapy for cardiovascular risk reduction
medicalTreatments.description: Adults with prediabetes often have concurrent dyslipidaemia; moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended by ACC/AHA guidelines for those with 10-year ASCVD risk ≥ 7.5%. Statins do modestly increase fasting glucose and HbA1c, but the cardiovascular benefit markedly outweighs the small diabetes risk in all risk groups.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces major cardiovascular events (heart attack, stroke, cardiovascular death) by approximately 25-35% per mmol/L reduction in LDL cholesterol in primary prevention, including in individuals with metabolic syndrome.

medicalTreatments.name: Antihypertensive therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Hypertension affects approximately 70% of people with prediabetes; ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred where concurrent microalbuminuria is present, as they provide renal protection beyond blood pressure lowering. Blood pressure target in prediabetes with cardiovascular risk is generally below 130/80 mmHg.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce the development of microalbuminuria by approximately 30% and reduce cardiovascular events; a 10 mmHg reduction in systolic blood pressure reduces major cardiovascular events by approximately 20-25%.

surgicalOptions.name: Bariatric and metabolic surgery
surgicalOptions.description: For severely obese adults (BMI ≥ 40, or ≥ 35 with significant comorbidities) who have prediabetes and have not responded adequately to lifestyle intervention, bariatric surgery — Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, or adjustable gastric band — achieves large, sustained weight losses and can normalise glucose metabolism in the majority of patients with prediabetes or early type 2 diabetes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution or remission of impaired glucose regulation in approximately 80-90% of patients with prediabetes or early type 2 diabetes following Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; remission sustained in approximately 60-70% at 5 years.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary approaches (Mediterranean, low-carbohydrate, DASH)
alternativeTreatments.description: Mediterranean, low-glycaemic index, and DASH dietary patterns consistently improve fasting glucose, HbA1c, and insulin sensitivity in prediabetes. A Mediterranean diet reduced diabetes incidence by approximately 52% vs. a low-fat control in the PREDIMED-Plus trial subsample.

alternativeTreatments.name: Resistance exercise and high-intensity interval training (HIIT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Resistance training independently improves insulin sensitivity and glucose disposal in muscle, additive to aerobic exercise. Two to three sessions per week of progressive resistance exercise are included in ADA prevention guidelines. HIIT achieves metabolic benefits in less time than traditional moderate-intensity aerobic exercise.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sleep optimisation and CPAP for sleep apnoea
alternativeTreatments.description: Treating obstructive sleep apnoea with CPAP modestly reduces fasting glucose and insulin resistance and is recommended as an adjunct in patients with confirmed sleep apnoea and prediabetes.

whySeeSpecialist: Most prediabetes is managed in primary care. Endocrinology or diabetology referral is appropriate when lifestyle intervention and metformin fail to prevent progression, when diabetes is confirmed, when unusual features suggest a secondary cause of hyperglycaemia (e.g. steroid-induced, MODY, pancreatic disease), or when severe obesity merits bariatric surgery evaluation. A dietitian, certified diabetes educator, exercise physiologist, and cardiologist may each contribute to a multidisciplinary team for high-risk patients.
doctorSelectionGuide: A primary care physician or internist with an interest in preventive metabolic medicine is the appropriate first clinician. Look for a practice that offers or refers to a CDC-recognised Diabetes Prevention Programme. For bariatric surgery evaluation, a bariatric surgery centre experienced in metabolic outcomes is required. For women with PCOS-related prediabetes, a reproductive endocrinologist or gynaecologist with metabolic interest adds value.
preventionStrategies: Achieve and maintain a body weight in the healthy range; a 5-10% reduction in weight is the single most impactful preventive step.
Accumulate at least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity such as brisk walking, cycling, or swimming, distributed across most days of the week.
Follow a dietary pattern rich in vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and lean protein while limiting refined carbohydrates, sugar-sweetened beverages, and saturated fat.
Prioritise 7-9 hours of sleep per night; get tested and treated for obstructive sleep apnoea if suspected.
Undergo screening for prediabetes at age 45 or earlier if overweight with risk factors; early detection expands the window for effective intervention.
Manage blood pressure and cholesterol to reduce cardiovascular risk, which is independently elevated in prediabetes even before diabetes develops.
lifestyleModifications: Reduce total calorie intake modestly — a deficit of 500-750 calories per day produces a sustainable weight loss rate of 0.5-1 kg per week.
Replace refined grains and starchy foods with non-starchy vegetables, legumes, and whole grains to reduce postprandial glucose spikes.
Eliminate sugar-sweetened beverages including fruit juices; replace with water, unsweetened tea, or sparkling water.
Incorporate brief movement breaks during prolonged sitting; even 2-5 minutes of light walking every 30 minutes improves postprandial glucose disposal.
Use a smaller plate and slow-paced eating to reduce calorie intake; eat breakfast to reduce large postprandial glucose excursions later in the day.
Maintain a food and activity journal for the first 3-6 months of intervention; self-monitoring consistently predicts weight loss success.
Involve family members or household members in dietary changes to create an environment that supports healthy eating.

recommended: Non-starchy vegetables (leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, courgette) at every meal to increase fibre, micronutrients, and satiety without raising glucose
Whole grains (oats, quinoa, barley, whole wheat) over refined grains — the fibre slows glucose absorption and improves postprandial glycaemia
Legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans) as a protein and fibre source with a low glycaemic index
Nuts and seeds (almonds, walnuts, chia, flaxseed) — their combination of protein, fat, and fibre blunts postprandial glucose and improves lipid profiles
Fatty fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) twice a week for omega-3 fatty acids that reduce triglycerides and cardiovascular risk
Olive oil and avocado as primary fat sources; their monounsaturated fat content improves insulin sensitivity
Plain yoghurt and fermented dairy — some evidence links dairy fermentation products to modestly reduced diabetes risk
avoid: Sugar-sweetened beverages including fruit juices — a single 355 mL serving raises blood glucose substantially and contributes significant caloric intake without satiety
Refined carbohydrates (white bread, white rice, processed breakfast cereals, pastries) that cause rapid glycaemic spikes
Ultra-processed foods high in added sugar, refined starch, and saturated fat
Excessive red meat and processed meat (sausage, bacon, deli meats) which are associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk independent of total calorie intake
Large portions of tropical high-sugar fruit (mango, pineapple, ripe banana) in favour of low-glycaemic berries and citrus
Alcohol above moderate intake (maximum 1 drink/day for women, 2/day for men) — excess alcohol adds calories and impairs judgement about food choices

exerciseGuidelines: ADA and AHA guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling, swimming), distributed over at least 3 days with no more than 2 consecutive days without activity. Adding 2-3 sessions per week of resistance training further improves insulin sensitivity and HbA1c and builds metabolically active muscle mass. Even brief bouts of standing or light walking after meals measurably reduce postprandial glucose levels. The total amount of activity matters more than the intensity for glucose control; starting with 10-15 minute sessions and gradually increasing duration is a practical approach for sedentary individuals. Those with co-existing cardiovascular disease or significant musculoskeletal problems should seek medical clearance before starting vigorous exercise.
dailyManagement: Check weight weekly and record it — weekly self-weighing consistently supports weight loss maintenance.
If prescribed metformin, take it with meals to reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and attend annual blood tests for renal function and vitamin B12.
Carry a low-calorie snack if you are susceptible to energy dips between meals that drive poor food choices.
Use a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) if your clinician agrees — CGM feedback improves dietary choices and motivation in prediabetes even before insulin is needed.
Review your medications annually with your doctor; if new drugs known to worsen glucose (steroids, antipsychotics) are prescribed, ensure glucose monitoring is increased.
Follow up with your GP or practice nurse at least annually for HbA1c or fasting glucose to catch progression to type 2 diabetes early.
prognosis: The prognosis for prediabetes is significantly better than commonly assumed. With structured intensive lifestyle intervention, approximately 58% of individuals avoid progression to type 2 diabetes over 3 years, and a meaningful proportion return to normal glucose regulation — estimated at 20-30% in DPP participants at 1 year. At 10 years, the DPP Outcomes Study showed a 34% sustained risk reduction for diabetes in the lifestyle group. Even participants who eventually progressed to diabetes did so approximately 3-4 years later in the lifestyle group than in placebo, which compresses the total period of hyperglycaemia and its cumulative complications. The risk of developing prediabetes-associated cardiovascular complications — largely driven by insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidaemia — is also significantly reduced by the same lifestyle measures. The key determinants of prognosis are the degree of weight loss achieved and sustained, physical activity level, and baseline beta-cell reserve as reflected by the HbA1c level within the prediabetic range.
recoveryTimeline: Fasting glucose and HbA1c may improve measurably within 4-8 weeks of significant dietary change and weight loss. Clinically meaningful HbA1c reduction of 0.3-0.5% occurs after 3-6 months of sustained lifestyle intervention. Weight loss of 5-7% of body weight — approximately 5-10 kg in most adults — is the target to achieve metabolic benefits; this can be accomplished within 3-6 months with a structured programme. Annual blood tests track progress and detect progression to diabetes. The DPP programme itself spans one year of intensive intervention followed by ongoing maintenance support.
complications: Progression to type 2 diabetes — 5-10% of people with prediabetes convert each year; cumulative 10-year risk is approximately 50-70% without intervention.
Cardiovascular disease — prediabetes independently raises the risk of myocardial infarction and stroke by approximately 15-20% compared to normoglycaemia, even before diabetes is diagnosed.
Early microvascular damage — retinopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and microalbuminuria can begin in the prediabetic range when average glucose is still below the diabetes threshold.

Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) — insulin resistance drives hepatic fat accumulation; NAFLD affects approximately 70% of individuals with type 2 diabetes and a substantial proportion with prediabetes.
Cognitive decline — emerging evidence links prediabetes to a modestly increased risk of dementia and accelerated cognitive ageing, though the mechanisms are not fully established.

supportResources.name: CDC — National Diabetes Prevention Program
supportResources.description: Find a CDC-recognised Diabetes Prevention Program near you or online — the evidence-based lifestyle intervention that reduces diabetes risk by 58%.

supportResources.name: American Diabetes Association — Prediabetes
supportResources.description: ADA patient resources on understanding prediabetes, risk factors, screening, and the steps to prevent progression to type 2 diabetes.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Prediabetes and Insulin Resistance
supportResources.description: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases plain-English overview of prediabetes, insulin resistance, and prevention strategies.

sources.title: Knowler WC et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. Diabetes Prevention Program Research Group. NEJM, 2002

Diabetes Prevention Program Outcomes Study Research Group. Long-term effects of lifestyle intervention or metformin on diabetes development and microvascular complications. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol, 2015
American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes — 2024. Diabetes Care, 2024
IDF Diabetes Atlas 10th Edition, 2021. International Diabetes Federation.
Tuomilehto J et al. Prevention of type 2 diabetes mellitus by changes in lifestyle among subjects with impaired glucose tolerance. Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study. NEJM, 2001

confusedWithConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type 2 diabetes is diagnosed when fasting glucose reaches 126 mg/dL or above, HbA1c reaches 6.5% or above, or 2-hour glucose reaches 200 mg/dL or above; prediabetes falls below these thresholds. The distinction determines whether glucose-lowering medications are needed and the intensity of monitoring.

confusedWithConditions.name: Type 1 diabetes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune destruction of beta cells that typically presents acutely with marked hyperglycaemia, polyuria, weight loss, and often ketoacidosis. Prediabetes is a slow, typically asymptomatic metabolic state driven by insulin resistance, not autoimmune beta-cell loss.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of five cardiovascular risk factors (central obesity, high triglycerides, low HDL, hypertension, and impaired fasting glucose); prediabetes specifically refers to the glycaemic component. The two conditions often co-exist but prediabetes can be present without the full metabolic syndrome cluster.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic ovary syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS is a hormonal disorder in women characterised by irregular periods, androgen excess, and polycystic ovaries; it is associated with significant insulin resistance and prediabetes as a complication, but PCOS and prediabetes are distinct diagnoses that may or may not co-exist.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Prediabetes is the immediate precursor to type 2 diabetes; most adults who develop type 2 diabetes have passed through an identifiable prediabetic phase.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Metabolic syndrome shares the same pathophysiological core — insulin resistance and central obesity — and frequently co-exists with prediabetes, increasing cardiovascular risk multiplicatively.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Early retinopathy changes can begin in the prediabetic range; baseline retinal screening is increasingly recommended for individuals with prediabetes and concurrent cardiovascular risk factors.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Microalbuminuria can develop in prediabetes before overt diabetes, making urine albumin monitoring part of the baseline evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary artery disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Prediabetes independently raises cardiovascular risk; coronary artery disease management must account for glycaemic status as part of total risk reduction.

faqs.question: What is prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Prediabetes is a blood glucose level that is higher than normal but not yet high enough to be diagnosed as type 2 diabetes. It is defined by a fasting glucose of 100-125 mg/dL, an HbA1c of 5.7-6.4%, or a 2-hour glucose of 140-199 mg/dL after a glucose drink. About 96 million American adults have it, and most do not know.

faqs.question: Can prediabetes be reversed?
faqs.answer: Yes. With structured lifestyle changes — losing 5-10% of body weight and exercising at least 150 minutes per week — approximately 58% of people with prediabetes avoid progression to type 2 diabetes. A significant proportion return to normal glucose levels. The earlier intervention starts, the greater the benefit.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Most people with prediabetes have no symptoms at all, which is why screening is essential. When symptoms do appear they can include increased thirst, fatigue, frequent urination, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds, and dark skin patches at the back of the neck or armpits. These symptoms are often mild and mistakenly attributed to other causes.

faqs.question: How is prediabetes diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Prediabetes is diagnosed with a blood test: a fasting glucose of 100-125 mg/dL, an HbA1c of 5.7-6.4%, or a 2-hour glucose of 140-199 mg/dL on an oral glucose tolerance test. An abnormal result should be confirmed with a second test on a different day unless two concordant tests are done simultaneously.

faqs.question: Who should be screened for prediabetes?
faqs.answer: The ADA recommends screening all adults aged 45 and over, and younger adults who are overweight or obese with at least one risk factor such as a family history of type 2 diabetes, gestational diabetes, high blood pressure, PCOS, or belonging to a high-risk ethnic group. Screening is repeated every 3 years if normal.

faqs.question: Does prediabetes always progress to diabetes?
faqs.answer: No. About 5-10% of people with prediabetes progress to type 2 diabetes each year without intervention, but with a structured lifestyle programme that number falls to roughly 2-5%. Many people remain in the prediabetic range for years, and some return to normal glucose levels. Active prevention is highly effective.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Prioritise reducing sugar-sweetened beverages (including juice), refined carbohydrates such as white bread, white rice, and pastries, ultra-processed foods, and large portions of starchy foods. These cause rapid glucose spikes. Replace them with non-starchy vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and lean protein.

faqs.question: Does exercise help prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, significantly. At least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise, spread across most days, improves insulin sensitivity within days of starting. Adding resistance training two to three times a week provides additional benefits. Even brief walks after meals measurably reduce postprandial glucose levels.

faqs.question: What is the HbA1c for prediabetes?
faqs.answer: An HbA1c between 5.7% and 6.4% (39-47 mmol/mol) indicates prediabetes according to ADA criteria. Below 5.7% is normal; 6.5% or above on two separate occasions indicates type 2 diabetes. HbA1c reflects your average blood glucose over the previous 2-3 months and does not require fasting.

faqs.question: Should I take metformin for prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Metformin is an option for some people with prediabetes, particularly those under 60 with a BMI over 35 or who have failed lifestyle modification. In the Diabetes Prevention Program, metformin reduced diabetes risk by 31% — less than the 58% achieved with intensive lifestyle change. Lifestyle modification is always the first-line approach.

faqs.question: Is prediabetes the same as metabolic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Not exactly. Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of five cardiovascular risk factors — central obesity, high triglycerides, low HDL cholesterol, high blood pressure, and impaired fasting glucose. Prediabetes refers specifically to the glucose component. The two conditions often overlap: many people with prediabetes also meet metabolic syndrome criteria.

faqs.question: Can children get prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rising childhood obesity rates mean prediabetes is increasingly diagnosed in adolescents and children. The ADA recommends testing overweight young people from age 10 or at puberty if they have two or more risk factors such as family history of type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, PCOS, or belonging to a high-risk ethnic group.

faqs.question: How often should I have blood tests with prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Annual testing with fasting glucose or HbA1c is recommended for all people with prediabetes. If you are actively working on lifestyle changes and your levels are improving, your doctor may extend intervals slightly. If your HbA1c is approaching 6.4%, more frequent monitoring is prudent to detect diabetes early.

faqs.question: Does prediabetes cause heart disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prediabetes independently raises the risk of cardiovascular disease — heart attack and stroke — by approximately 15-20% compared to normal glucose, even before diabetes develops. This is partly because insulin resistance drives other metabolic risk factors including high triglycerides, low HDL cholesterol, and high blood pressure, which together add up to substantially elevated cardiovascular risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance?
faqs.answer: Impaired fasting glucose (IFG) is an elevated glucose after an overnight fast (100-125 mg/dL) reflecting mainly hepatic insulin resistance. Impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) is an elevated glucose 2 hours after a glucose drink (140-199 mg/dL) reflecting mainly peripheral insulin resistance. IGT carries higher cardiovascular risk; having both conditions doubles the annual diabetes progression rate.

faqs.question: Can losing weight cure prediabetes?
faqs.answer: Losing 5-10% of body weight — typically 5-12 kg in most adults — frequently normalises fasting glucose and HbA1c in the prediabetic range, a state sometimes called diabetes remission or reversal. Whether this constitutes a cure depends on whether weight is maintained: prediabetes typically returns if weight is regained. Sustained modest weight loss is the most effective long-term strategy.

faqs.question: Is prediabetes a disability?
faqs.answer: Prediabetes itself is not typically classified as a disability under the ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) unless it significantly limits major life activities. However, significant progression and comorbidities associated with prediabetes — neuropathy, cardiovascular disease, vision problems — can reach disability thresholds. Always discuss workplace accommodations with an occupational health clinician if needed.

faqs.question: What is the Diabetes Prevention Program?
faqs.answer: The CDC-recognised Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) is a structured 12-month lifestyle intervention delivered in small group sessions by trained facilitators. It targets at least 7% weight loss and 150 minutes of weekly physical activity. It has been shown to reduce diabetes incidence by 58% and is covered by Medicare and many private insurers in the United States.

faqs.question: Can prediabetes cause neuropathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Peripheral neuropathy — tingling, numbness, or burning in the feet and hands — can develop in the prediabetic range, before overt diabetes is diagnosed. This is because even modestly elevated glucose and insulin resistance can damage small nerve fibres over years. Prediabetic neuropathy responds to the same glucose-lowering lifestyle changes that prevent diabetes progression.

faqs.question: What percentage of prediabetes turns into diabetes?
faqs.answer: Without intervention, approximately 5-10% of people with prediabetes progress to type 2 diabetes each year. Over 10 years, cumulative risk is approximately 50-70% depending on initial glucose level and other risk factors. With intensive lifestyle intervention, the annual conversion rate drops to roughly 2-5%, and metformin reduces it to approximately 3-7%.

metaDescription: Prediabetes affects 96 million Americans and raises diabetes and heart risk. Learn blood glucose thresholds, lifestyle reversal, and when to start medication.
